sentences.text: Yes.
We brought him to camp.
The next day, they took him away to the Ukrainian police.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The following day, they called the whole camp into what you call an appell place.
They gathered the whole camp.
Nobody went to work.
Everybody was gathered together.
And a very high Gestapo SS man, a very high-ranking officer by the name of Hildenbrand-- which, by the way, was tried after the war.
I don't know what-- I think he got 10 years.
Oh, that's H-I-L-D-E-N-B-R-A-N-D.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Had a great speech for us, how we shouldn't be terrorists and walk around with guns and go to the forest and so on and so forth.
At that point, they brought this fellow in, this young fellow in, and another one.
They were beaten up so badly that their faces were completely black and blue.
And they put them up against the wall.
And two SS men, one by the name of Messinger-- you would spell his name M-E-- double-S-- I-N-G-E-R.
The other guy-- and that's, by the way, the same guy that caught me on the street.
The other SS man, I don't remember his name.
And they shot both of the boys in the back of their neck.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took them next courtyard.
They dug two graves.
And they buried them.
And one of the boys was still alive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that young SS man jumped over the fence quickly and finished him off.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After that--
He shot him again.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After that-- that must have been sometime end of May or beginning of June.
On June 22, the camp was surrounded by the Germans.
And everybody-- most everybody because a few people had hiding places, and they hid.
But very few.
And that was so-called the second transport from that war camp to Plaszow.
Of course, my sister and my father and myself were in between.
They were taken-- we were taken to the train station, loaded into wagons, and were taken to Plaszow, the concentration camp Plaszow.

The ride was maybe four or five days because even though the distance wasn't that great, but they used to put us always on the side railings when they needed the main rails for the transport of their soldiers or whatever.
Well, that train was still-- wasn't that horrible because we were still quite strong.
And we survived till that point.
And we weren't-- there was no malnutrition at that point set in.
And we got to the Plaszow camp.
We were put in the quarantine for a few days.
Of course, then they took away everything, whatever anybody brought with them.
And they took all their clothes away.
They put you into the-- into a bath.
They shaved you all over, your head and your-- everywhere.
And they sprayed disinfectant on you.
And then we were put into barracks.
The women were separate.
The men were separate.
I was with my father.
And my father, of course, started working in a cabinet-making shop.
And I was schlepping after him.
I was the assistant helper.
We were working there for a few weeks.
One night, one evening, they called a few fellows out to appell.
Appellplatz.
Appellplatz is a big space where every day in the morning and in the evening, the whole camp had to stand on attention.
They were counting the people.
God forbid somebody shouldn't escape.
And they were count-- we had to stand on attention as long till their count was right.
But that evening, they took just a few people out.
I was one of them.
I don't remember why.
A friend of mine that lives in this building, Igor [?
Klink ?], was with me, too.

He was also as lucky [? as me. ?] We were taken to dig out mass graves, take the people out that were buried for about three years already, put them on the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we used to put them on-- on wood that the whole camp had to gather together.
They used to-- after work, they used to march the whole camp, which may be 20,000 people.
And everybody had to bring a piece of wood.
Where they got it from, I don't know.
And we used to dig out the people, put it on a stretcher, walk out from the mass grave, where we saw-- the grave was dug out in a [INAUDIBLE], where the people were preserved to such a degree.
They were all naked, but if there was a piece of cloth somewhere, you could see the colors of it.
You could see mothers with their children in their arms.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We had to walk up.
An SS man was standing there with a pair of pliers and used to examine every head.
And where there ever was any gold, he used to pull it out.
And we threw those dead bodies on a-- on a mass point.
And after a while, they used to pour gasoline on it and burn it.
After they burned it, we used to take the bones and had big rollers, like your roll the street with, the asphalt.
We used to crush the bones.
There shouldn't be no sign of anything that the Germans did, that they killed people, when the-- because the Russians were marching east-- westward already.
And that's how we had to destroy all the evidence of their cruelty.
And that's how I worked for 10 nights with Igor, digging out dead bodies from mass graves and burning them. 10 nights, which is like 100 years.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
All right.
After that, it was-- it's a wonder that they didn't liquidate us because they usually used to shoot people after something like that.
But they didn't at that point.
And sometimes in October, we were sent away to another camp by the name of Gross-Rosen.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Want me to spell it for you?
G-R-O-- double-S-- hyphen-- R-O-S-E-N.
This camp till today is not very famous.
Very few people heard of it.
I think it was the one of the most cruelest camps that you can imagine.
That was already in Germany.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was one of the cruelest camps that you could imagine.
If anybody would invented what they did with us, you couldn't.
First when we got there, it was late October, I think.
They took us, we-- took us to a big place.
We-- everybody had to undress, throw everything away, whatever we brought with us.
We went through a-- they checked us out.
They even checked your rectum.
And we stood a whole night on the outside, in the drizzling rain, naked, standing and waiting, I don't know for what.
The next day in the morning, they took us to the showers.
First, they shaved us again.
They put disinfectants on.
And then, naked, they issued us shirts, just long shirts.
And they took us to a barrack.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember how many hundreds of us we were, maybe 500 people in one big room.
We had to sit one squashed into another because there was no room to stand up or to lie down.
And if anybody had to go to the bathroom, it was just-- he had to step over everybody to get out.
He could never get back in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next day, they issued some kind of clothes to us, and they started practicing marching and standing at attention and taking your hats off.
And if you had a row of 200 people, and if one head went a fraction of a second not at the same time as everybody else, it was hell to pay.
They could put us kneeling down-- not kneeling down, but squatting, for hours and hours.
And if you fell, they beat you to death.
And-- what they were doing to us was unbelievable.
At that time, some people from Camp Plaszow were under Schindler's list.
They were all taken to Gross-Rosen with us together.
But there they were separated and taken away by Schindler.
And they were the lucky ones.
I, unfortunately, didn't have that luck.
My friend Igor was very lucky.
He was on Schindler's list.
And they went.
They stayed in one camp, and they survived the war there.
I, on the other hand, even at Gross-Rosen, I stayed there a few days longer.
And we had to carry rocks, run with a rock up the hill and down the hill, up the hill and down the hill.
And there was no end to their cruelty.
There was no end.
Yes, we received some food.
How much, I don't remember.
They usually used to give us black coffee or black water in the morning with a piece of bread, and then in the evening a little soup and a piece of bread.
I-- after Gross-Rosen, I was taken for a transport to Buchenwald.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That trip took about four or five days.
We had no food.
We were locked up in cattle trains.
We had no food at all, no water and no food.
At one point, the German soldiers that were guarding us were very nice to us.
And they brought us white beets, which were sugar beets.
And of course, we ate that because we had nothing else, and we were dying of thirst because no water.
That was very sweet.
And that's how they played with us.
And--
Well, there must have been civilians while passing by all those towns.
I-- we couldn't see any because we had no windows.
There was a little window on top of the-- of each wagon, which was maybe two foot by one foot high.
And that was the only-- so you couldn't look out, and you couldn't see anything.
And besides, there were Germans and Ukrainians watching the train all over.
So you couldn't stick your head out or anything like that.
And we got to Buchenwald.
I was there--
I think on that trip, everybody survived.
Yes.

And I-- I can say only about the wagon I was in because once you arrived at the camp, they opened up the door-- and like you see, really, in the movies sometimes, when you watch those movies, they yelled.
They screamed.
They have the dogs.
They push you.
They-- they shove you.
So you cannot look around.
You cannot talk to anybody.
You cannot see what's happening.
You just run in order they shouldn't hit you, they shouldn't beat you.
Because beating was very terrible.
I was always very scared of beating.
We got to Buchenwald.
We were there a short time.
And we were taken to a commando-- a camp that belonged to Buchenwald, a camp by the name of Ohrdruf.
This, as a matter of fact, is the first camp that General Eisenhower marched in with his American army.
That was the first camp he saw.
I was in that camp for a while.
I was working.
They used to take us at night.
I was very lucky to work at night always, somehow.
We were digging tunnels in a mountain where they were still planning to put factories in, even though they the war-- the war was almost at the end.
That was already 1944.
Yeah, end of 1944.
And they were still planning to build factories on-- on the-- And the work was very hard, very brutal.
It was very cold.
It was winter.
And I saw that I will not survive.
There was one barrack there in the camp where they kept people too sick to work.
But they almost got as much food as the people that worked.
So I thought to myself, I'd rather survive there if I'm going to survive.
Without working, I have a better chance and a little food than working because I'll drop dead.
And I somehow volunteered to go to that camp, which was dangerous, too, because they could take you out to the crematorium at any time.
People that didn't work had no value at all.
Even people that worked didn't have much of a value, but especially people that didn't work.
Anyway, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at that time, I was always with my father.
I got-- I got separated with him in Buchenwald.
And I volunteered to go to that barrack with the sick people.
And I was there for a couple days.
And suddenly, my father showed up from somewhere.
I don't even know where he was.
And we were reunited together.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In that place, I was still doing pretty good physically.
And I started helping out the guy that was in charge of the barrack because we had German prisoners.
Some were criminals, some were political.
They were in concentration camps.
But they always were kapos, and they were always in charge of something.
And they had it pretty good.
I started helping him out with the-- handing out the soup and so on.
So there was always a little extra soup left over that they shared with myself and my father.
And-- but there was no future there to survive at all.
And they were calling for a transport to go someplace else.
My father and myself volunteered, and we went.
And that must have been, like, I'd say, December 31 or January 1 of 1945 already.
We went to Sachsenhausen.
Sachsenhausen was a very big camp, tremendously big camp.
It was a showcase of the great German republic.
They used to bring Red Cross people there.
They used to bring [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people from Switzerland to examine the camps.
It was tremendous camp.
When we arrived there, I was sick.
And I have such a nature that I can feel whenever I have temperature.
When we arrived there, we were separated or segregated, who was going where.
And I told the German, the SS man, that I don't feel good, that I'm sick.
Must have been crazy.
He gave me a thermometer.
And, of course, I had high temperature.
And by a miracle, they had a hospital in camp.
They put me in the hospital.
Later, I found out I had typhus.
I was for three days unconscious, completely.
I don't remember the three days.
But I was told later that it was a Russian doctor there, also a prisoner, that was giving me some injections and helping me out.
This in any other camp, would have never survived.
In that hospital were mostly Poles and other nationalities.
I don't remember any Jew.
Anyway, as I was recuperating there, one day they were handing out packages from the Red Cross.
So, of course, I had my handout, too.
But some people started saying, he's a Jew.
And I didn't get it, which was not that important.
I was in a warm, clean place.
And I was getting some food, I guess, to survive.
And I didn't have to work.
It was pretty good.
Then one day they sent you out on a transport walking because it was already January.
The Russians were pushing them back.
So they had to-- Sachsenhausen, I think, was under Berlin or some-- near Berlin.
Anyway, I said to the guy there-- there was no German anymore.
It was whatever was in charge-- I can't go because I still have temperature.
And truthfully, I did it.
And they left me alone.
After a few days, we all went to Bergen-Belsen.
That trip, at least I didn't have to walk.
You know, those walking trips, that transport that went walking, maybe 10% survived because they walked you for days and days.
It was impossible.
Anyway, I got to Bergen-Belsen, and there already was-- already, the Germans didn't have their records straight anymore.
There was already running from one camp to other, different parts of camps.
And I almost separated myself from the Jews.
And there was one barrack with Danes and-- and somebody else.
And so I tried to stick to them.
And I met-- I met a German that came from Silesia.
And he spoke Polish, and he spoke German.
And he didn't know I was Jewish.
And he says, look, you stick with me.
I'll help you out and this and that.
And when they're looking for a transport, why don't you volunteer?
And I volunteered again.
That's how I used to go from camp to camp.
I could never-- in some camps, people went to work in a factory.
And they didn't have it that bad.
And I could never get to anything where I could stay in one place and-- and work at something.
I always would shovel and a pick or-- or whatever it was.
I never had anything decent.
I mean, that-- in a warm place, like in a factory, that I could work.
Anyway, we went on the transport.
We went to Hamburg.
Hamburg, we arrived.
They took us to a big place.
And as we arrived, I saw a big plaque there.
Where they-- it was written Speicher on it, exactly the way I spell my name.
And that's where the camp was, and-- which later, I find-- found out, was the silos.
At the canal in Hamburg were silos.
That's where we were located.
